title: Transcribing data
text: MiMo is designed to work with Minimal Input. So all you need to do is transcribe using standard orthography (Roman Alphabet).

title: Transcribing data
text: MiMo is designed to work with Minimal Input. So all you need to do is transcribe using standard orthography (Roman Alphabet).

title: Transcribing data
text:
Sentence-final punctuation
Every line should end with an sentence-final punctuation, e.g. a full stop (.), question mark (?), or exclamation mark (!). MiMo uses sentence-final punctuation to determine when a sentence / utterance ends, and a new one begins. So including sentence-final punctuation is critical

title: Transcribing data
text:
Showing speakers
Speakers should be shown with a series of letters followed by a colon (:). There should be no spaces, or punctuation characters. So you could type FAT:, FATHER:, Father:, FATHEROFCHILD:, FatherOfChild:
If you would like to include multiple words in the speaker name, you could use “camel case”

title: Transcribing data
text:
Punctuation
Use standard punctuation. Show contractions with an apostrophe, e.g. we're having fun, I've eaten already. This will analyse we're as a single word containing two morphemes. If you want MiMo to analyse as two words you would need to write the words out in full, e.g. we are.
You can add other types of punctuation, e.g. commas and dashes. They will be ignored when conducting the linguistic analysis.

title: Transcribing data
text:
Return characters
MiMo does not pay attention to return ⏎ characters. So, the following text fragment, which contains lots of return characters
MOT: Can you tell what we're cooking ? ⏎ 
MOT: Can you smell it ? ⏎ 
CHI: Pasta and chips . ⏎ 
MOT: Yes . ⏎ 
MOT: It's not chips . ⏎ 
MOT: It's pasta and we've got bacon . ⏎ 
CHI: Oh thanks . ⏎ 
will be treated the same as the following text fragment where the same text is written on a single line.
MOT: Can you tell what we're cooking ? Can you smell it ? CHI: Pasta and chips . MOT: Yes . It's not chips . MOT: It's pasta and we've got bacon . CHI: Oh thanks .
Note that it’s not necessary to always specify the speaker. If no speaker is specified at the beginning of the sentence, then MiMo will assume that the sentence is a continuation of the conversational turn of the previous speaker. This should greatly help to simplify transcription.

title: Transcribing data
text:
Comments
If you want to include text which does not belong to a speaker you can use comments. These involve placing text inside round brackets, as in the example above. MiMo will not assign word classes to text in round brackets, and will not include this text in the various metrics which it calculates (Mean Length of Utterance, and various lexical metrics)

title: Automatic language detection
text:
Once you have entered some text, MiMo automatically tries to detect the language. It needs to do this so it can ‘parse’ the text. This involves analysing its structure, and assigning part-of-speech tags. It uses an algorithm which prioritises speed over accuracy. The more text you input the more accurate the analysis.
For short fragments of text, it may get things spectacularly wrong. It sometimes analyses English as Middle Frisian! If this happens, all you have to do is specify the language in the textbox as shown below.

title: About
text: About this site

title: Automatic language detection
text:
Morphological analysis in English
Morphological analysis is coded for English only. This is via a two stage process;

Consult the universal dependency part of speech tags for information regarding grammatical feature with morphological consequences, e.g. past tense, progressive aspect etc.
If a particular grammatical feature is present, e.g. past, search for the regular morpheme on the word in question.

This strategy is accurate for English as morphology is highly regular, and is also othographically coded in a regular fashion (e.g. all regular English words, when written, end in -ed).
This is quite of lot of work, so I have not done this for any other languages.






Note



The code to morphologically tag English is introduced by an RStudio bookmark called # English labelling rules ----

title: Automatic language detection
text:
A workaround for non-English languages
For non-English languages you can delimit morphemes using word-internal punctuation, e.g. we’re having a nice time / esta-mos pasandolo bien (SPANISH).
The word class analysis overrides the word-internal punctuation, parsing it as one word, rather than two.

title: Entering data
text:
Just copy and paste your data into the textbox. Be careful, when pasting from a word processor document (e.g. Microsoft Word), as this may insert unusual characters (e.g. tab and return characters) which may affect the processing of the data. Ideally you should be transcribing your data in the plain text (.txt) format, which is supported by apps such as Notepad for Windows, and TextEdit for MacOS (Apple). Apps used by programmers for writing code, e.g. Sublime Text, and Visual Studio are also good for writing plain text files.



You also have the option to upload the data in plain text format.




That’s fine, as long as you copy and paste the text into the textbox, rather than uploading the file.

title: Why I created a new app
text:
Finding “the sweet spot”
There are a wide range of language analysis apps designed for child language and clinical data, including CLAN, SALT and SUGAR. However, none of these hits the 🍰 sweet spot between power and usability. CLAN is an impressive app, but difficult to learn, and for this reason is used mainly by the research community. SUGAR is a quick and simple system designed for Speech and Language Therapists, but the therapist still has to do some work to calculate MLU, code target structures, and derive some norms. With MiMo the plan is to “get the best of both worlds” by combining the ease and simplicity of SUGAR with the power and flexibilty CLAN.
The name MiMo describes the philosophy of the app. “Mi” refers to minimal inputs. In other words, the app is easy to use, and requires very little training. “Mo” refers to maximal outputs. This refers to the rich data produced by the app, including 🌈 coloured word classes, and 📉 graphs of norms.
crumbs: Why I created a new app

title: Why I created a new app
text:
Making language analysis fun 😁!
Many apps for language analysis are quite mechanical in the way they work. You feed text in, and they produce a range of numerical metrics. There is little room for intellectual curiosity or intuition.
MiMo, buy contrast, allows the user to explore their data. You can quickly and easily search for particular word classes, or grammatical phenomena. You can quickly identify the longer and more complex structures that a participant produces. You can sort alphabetically to find rote-learned patterns that an individual is particularly dependent on. And these are just some of the things you can do!
crumbs: Why I created a new app

title: Colour highlights
text:
If MiMo is able to access a grammar for you language it will colour your text using different colours for different wordclasses

When you hover over the word, the word class will show in a box above the word.

The colours are designed to be meaningful. For example, all words related to the verb, e.g. verb, adverb, auxiliary verb are highlighted various shades of red / pink. I chose a bright colour because verbs are essential to sentences. There are many languages which have sentences consisting only of a verb, but few which have sentences consisting only of a noun.


If you do not like the beautiful colours I have chosen, you may alter them on the 🌈 Colours tab. Unfortunately, changes in colour do not persist across sessions, so if you close the browser, you still want to use your bespoke colours, you will need to redo them.
You can also choose sets of colours, e.g. Verb-related words only, or Linking words.

title: Colour highlights

title: Metrics
text:
Metrics
MiMo reports a range of metrics including

Mean Length of Utterance in Words
Mean Length of Utterance in Morphemes (provided MiMo is able to morphologically segement your language)
Type Token Ratio
VocD (HDD)

These may be accessed under the 🌈 Let's explore tab.

title: Exploring your data
text:
MiMo presents your data in an interactive table which provides wonderful new ways to explore your data.


When you filter the table it will show only rows where a particular search string occurs. You do this by typing characters in either (a) the empty boxes at the top of each column, or (b) the empty box right t the top of the table. The boxes at the top of each column are column filters and the search applies to that column only. The big box at the top is a universal filter. It searches all columns.
